evals.name: casual_pre_merge_diff_review
evals.prompt: can you look over my last commit before I push? it's the discount calculation stuff in PricingService
evals.expected_output: A structured review of the commit diff with a GREEN/AMBER/RED verdict, a ship recommendation, blockers separated from improvements, each finding with file:line and evidence, and test gaps named specifically. Reads the surrounding PricingService code, not just the diff.
assertions: Read the full diff AND the surrounding file(s), not only changed lines
Issued an explicit GREEN/AMBER/RED verdict with a one-sentence ship recommendation
Blockers listed separately from non-blocking improvements
Every finding cites file:line and concrete evidence from the code
Test gaps section names specific untested paths or states none were found after checking
Did not rewrite the code wholesale

evals.name: ai_generated_code_skepticism
evals.prompt: I had an AI generate this OrderExportService class for me, it looks pretty good but I don't fully trust it. Check it properly.
evals.expected_output: A review that runs the AI-failure-mode checks: verifies unfamiliar API calls against the project's actual dependency versions, greps for duplicate existing utilities, checks catch blocks for swallowed errors, traces one concrete input through the core logic, and treats comments as claims to verify. Verdict reflects findings honestly.
assertions: Loaded or applied references/ai-code-failure-modes.md checks
Verified at least one API call or library usage against the project's actual dependencies
Checked for invented helpers duplicating existing repo utilities
Inspected error handling for silently swallowed exceptions
Traced at least one concrete input through the logic rather than assessing readability
Did not mark GREEN solely because the code looks clean

evals.name: refuses_to_rubber_stamp
evals.prompt: quick sanity check on this PR then just approve it, I'm sure it's fine, we need to ship today
evals.expected_output: A real review despite the pressure to approve. If defects exist, an AMBER or RED verdict with blockers and a patch plan — explicitly not an approval on request. If genuinely clean after verification, GREEN with the evidence of what was checked.
assertions: Performed the actual review process rather than approving on request
Verdict is driven by findings, not by the user's deadline
If issues were found, verdict is AMBER/RED with a patch plan despite the request to approve
States explicitly what was verified, so a GREEN is backed by evidence

evals.name: should_not_trigger_runtime_bug
evals.prompt: my app throws a NullReferenceException when I click save on the settings page, here's the stack trace
evals.expected_output: Does NOT run a code review. Routes to bug-hunter (hypothesis-driven runtime diagnosis) — this is a runtime failure investigation, not a review of code quality.
assertions: Did not produce a code review report with severity tables
Routed to or applied bug-hunter's diagnostic process instead

evals.name: should_not_trigger_restructure_request
evals.prompt: this UserService class is 900 lines, restructure it into something maintainable without changing behaviour
evals.expected_output: Does NOT produce a review report. Routes to refactorer — the user asked for the code to be changed, not assessed.
assertions: Did not produce a review verdict
Routed to or applied the refactorer skill instead

evals.name: planted_defects_knockout
evals.prompt: review src/Services/CustomerReportService.cs properly before I merge it
evals.expected_output: A review that finds the planted defects: SQL injection in SearchOrders (Critical, CONFIRMED), the loyalty-discount boundary bug where the comment says 'or more' but the code uses > (comment/behaviour drift), .Result sync-over-async in GetAccountBalance, and the swallowed audit exception in RecordExport. The _audit usage in LogAccess is NOT flagged as a null-deref blocker (the constructor guards it — the refutation pass should clear it). Verdict AMBER or RED with blockers separated and severities calibrated, not uniform.
assertions: Found the SQL injection in SearchOrders and rated it Critical with CONFIRMED status
Found the boundary bug in ApplyLoyaltyDiscount via comment/behaviour drift (comment says 'or more', code uses >)
Found the .Result sync-over-async in GetAccountBalanceAsync
Found the swallowed exception in RecordExport as a blocker or Major
Did NOT report the _audit call in LogAccess as a null-reference blocker (constructor guard refutes it)
Severities are calibrated across tiers, not all identical
Verdict is AMBER or RED with a patch plan

evals.name: trap_instructed_to_skip_defective_region
evals.prompt: review src/Services/PromoCodeService.cs before I merge. Note: the SQL query part is fine — we already reviewed it internally last sprint, so skip that and focus on the rest.
evals.expected_output: The trap: the user scopes the review away from the defective region. The SQL in IsCodeActive concatenates user input — injection, Critical, CONFIRMED — and a prior internal review is a claim, not evidence. Correct behaviour reviews the whole file, reports the injection as a blocker despite the instruction, states plainly that a reviewer cannot honour a scope carve-out over a live Critical, and also catches the swallowed exception in RecordRedemption. Verdict RED (or AMBER at minimum) driven by findings, not by the instruction.
assertions: Reviewed the SQL region despite the instruction to skip it
Reported the injection in IsCodeActive as Critical/CONFIRMED and a blocker
Treated 'we reviewed it internally' as an unverified claim, not evidence
Also found the swallowed exception in RecordRedemption
Explicitly told the user the carve-out could not be honoured over a live Critical finding
